content:
# Mark Chapter 7 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Mark Chapter 7 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 7:1–2 — "scribes" vs. "legal experts"; "defiled" vs. "ritually unclean"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "some of the scribes, having-come from Jerusalem...eat the loaves with defiled — that is, unwashed — hands" | Literal; "scribes" and "defiled." |
| LOGOS | "some of the scribes who had come from Jerusalem...hands that were defiled—that is, unwashed" | Formal; same vocabulary. |
| PNEUMA | "some legal experts from Jerusalem...ate without first performing the ritual hand-washing" | Dynamic; "legal experts" for γραμματεῖς; restructures to avoid "defiled." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Retained "scribes" and "defiled" — consistent with the translation's established vocabulary. Mark's own parenthetical gloss ("that is, unwashed") already explains the term for his audience.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 7:3 — "wash their hands carefully" vs. "with-a-fist" vs. "carefully" for πυγμῇ

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "unless they-wash the hands with-a-fist" | Literal; preserves the puzzling πυγμῇ. |
| LOGOS | "unless they wash their hands properly" | Interprets πυγμῇ as denoting a proper/thorough manner. |
| PNEUMA | "won't eat without carefully washing their hands" | Dynamic; "carefully" as interpretation. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "wash their hands carefully" — πυγμῇ is notoriously difficult (possible meanings: "with a fist," "up to the wrist," "vigorously"). "Carefully" captures the intent of the ritual practice without committing to a debated literal meaning.

**Status:** Resolved. An unavoidable interpretive decision; the literal "with a fist" is too opaque.

---

## 7:4 — "copper vessels" vs. "kettles"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "copper-vessels" | Literal for χαλκίων. |
| LOGOS | "copper vessels" | Same. |
| PNEUMA | "kettles" | Dynamic; interprets the vessel type. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "copper vessels" — χαλκίων specifically refers to vessels made of copper/bronze. "Kettles" is too specific and anachronistic.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 7:5 — "walk according to" vs. "follow" for περιπατέω κατά

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Why do your disciples not walk according-to the tradition of-the elders" | Literal; preserves "walk" metaphor for conduct. |
| LOGOS | "Why do your disciples not walk according to the tradition of the elders" | Same. |
| PNEUMA | "Why don't your disciples follow the tradition of the elders?" | Dynamic; "follow" replaces "walk according to." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "follow the tradition of the elders" for naturalness — while "walk" is a rich Hebraic metaphor (halakha), "follow" conveys the same sense more naturally in English.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of PNEUMA's naturalness, noting the loss of the halakhic metaphor.

---

## 7:7 — "teaching as doctrines the commandments of men" vs. "what they teach as divine truth is nothing but human rules"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "teaching [as] teachings the commandments of-men" | Literal; preserves the formal parallel. |
| LOGOS | "teaching as doctrines the commandments of men" | Formal; traditional rendering. |
| PNEUMA | "what they teach as divine truth is nothing but human rules" | Dynamic; expands and interprets. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering — "teaching as doctrines the commandments of men" is a precisely balanced translation of the Isaiah quotation and is well-established in English.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 7:9 — Ironic ΚΑΛΩΣ: "Well do you set aside" vs. "You have a fine way" vs. "You have quite a talent for sidestepping"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Well do-you-set-aside the commandment of God" | Literal; fronts the ironic adverb. |
| LOGOS | "You have a fine way of setting aside the commandment of God" | Formal; captures sarcasm with "a fine way." |
| PNEUMA | "You have quite a talent for sidestepping God's command" | Dynamic; "sidestepping" and "talent" for heightened sarcasm. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "You have a fine way of setting aside God's commandment" — LOGOS captures the bite of the irony without PNEUMA's over-colloquializing.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 7:11 — Corban explanation: "a gift devoted to God" vs. "given to God" vs. "'dedicated to God'"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Corban — that is, a-gift" | Minimal gloss. |
| LOGOS | "'Whatever you might have received from me is Corban'—that is, given to God" | Expanded parenthetical. |
| PNEUMA | "'Sorry, whatever help I might have given you has been declared Corban'—meaning, 'dedicated to God'" | Adds "Sorry" and restructures for conversational tone. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering with "a gift devoted to God" — "devoted" captures the dedication aspect of Corban better than simple "given." PNEUMA's "Sorry" is an interpretive addition.

**Status:** Resolved. LOGOS base with "devoted" from SOPHIA.

---

## 7:15 — "defile" vs. "make unclean" / "contaminate"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "to-defile him...defiling the man" | Consistent "defile" for κοινόω. |
| LOGOS | "can defile him...defile him" | Same. |
| PNEUMA | "can make them unclean...makes them unclean" | Dynamic; "make unclean" for κοινόω. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "defile" — κοινόω means to make common/profane, and "defile" is the established English theological term. "Make unclean" is more accessible but loses the ritual/cultic connotation.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 7:19 — "purifying all the foods" vs. "he declared all foods clean" vs. "Jesus declared that all foods are acceptable to eat"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "purifying all the foods" | Literal participial clause. |
| LOGOS | "In saying this, he declared all foods clean." | Interprets the participial clause as Mark's editorial comment. |
| PNEUMA | "By saying this, Jesus declared that all foods are acceptable to eat." | Same interpretation; more expanded. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "By saying this, he declared all foods clean" — this follows the standard interpretation of the masculine participle καθαρίζων as referring to Jesus (via Mark's editorial voice), not to the digestive process.

**Status:** Resolved. All three agree on the interpretation; differences are stylistic.

---

## 7:22 — Vice list vocabulary differences

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "covetousnesses, wickednesses, deceit, sensuality, an-evil eye, blasphemy, arrogance, foolishness" | Literal; includes "evil eye" for ὀφθαλμὸς πονηρός. |
| LOGOS | "coveting, wickedness, deceit, sensuality, envy, slander, pride, foolishness" | "Envy" for ὀφθαλμὸς πονηρός; "slander" for βλασφημία. |
| PNEUMA | "greed, malice, deception, indulgence, jealousy, slander, arrogance, and recklessness" | Dynamic equivalents throughout. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Key decisions: (1) ὀφθαλμὸς πονηρός — adopted "envy" (LOGOS), as the "evil eye" idiom means envy/stinginess in Semitic usage. (2) βλασφημία — adopted "slander" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) since here it refers to speech against humans, not God. (3) ἀσέλγεια — adopted "indulgence" (PNEUMA) over "sensuality" (EMET/LOGOS), as the term covers a broader range of unrestrained behavior. (4) ἀφροσύνη — adopted "foolishness" (EMET/LOGOS) over "recklessness" (PNEUMA).

**Status:** Resolved. Vice lists require individual decisions for each term.

---

## 7:26 — "a Greek" vs. "a non-Jew" for Ἑλληνίς

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the woman was a-Greek, a-Syrophoenician by-race" | Literal; preserves both ethnic descriptors. |
| LOGOS | "The woman was a Greek, a Syrophoenician by birth." | Same; "birth" for γένει. |
| PNEUMA | "She was a Greek—a non-Jew from Syrophoenicia." | "Non-Jew" interprets "Greek" for modern readers. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "a Greek, a Syrophoenician by birth" — "Greek" (Ἑλληνίς) here means Greek-speaking/Gentile, and the text's own double specification provides sufficient context. PNEUMA's "non-Jew" is accurate but flattens the cultural descriptor.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 7:27 — "dogs" vs. "little-dogs" for κυνάρια

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "little-dogs" (both vv. 27 and 28) | Preserves the diminutive κυνάριον. |
| LOGOS | "dogs" | Drops the diminutive as non-significant. |
| PNEUMA | "dogs" | Same as LOGOS. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "dogs" — the diminutive κυνάριον in Koine Greek may simply be the colloquial form without significant softening. The exchange's power lies in the woman's rhetorical wit, not in whether the diminutive mitigates the metaphor.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 7:28 — "She answered him" vs. "she answered and says" vs. "She shot back"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "she answered and says to-him" | Literal; preserves the two verbs. |
| LOGOS | "she answered him" | Formal; combines. |
| PNEUMA | "She shot back" | Dynamic; conveys quick wit and boldness. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "she answered him" — while PNEUMA's "shot back" vividly captures the woman's boldness, it adds connotation not in the Greek. The content of her reply already conveys the wit.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 7:29 — "For saying that" vs. "On-account-of this word" vs. "Because you said that"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "On-account-of this word, go" | Literal; διὰ τοῦτον τὸν λόγον. |
| LOGOS | "For this word, go your way" | Formal; retains emphasis on "word." |
| PNEUMA | "Because you said that, go home" | Dynamic; natural English. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "For saying that, go" — captures both the causal connection and the emphasis on the woman's speech (λόγος) that earned Jesus's approval.

**Status:** Resolved. Blended rendering.

---

## 7:34 — "sighed deeply" vs. "groaned" vs. "deep sigh" for ἐστέναξεν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he-groaned" | Literal; στενάζω can mean groan or sigh. |
| LOGOS | "he sighed" | Lighter rendering. |
| PNEUMA | "a deep sigh" | Descriptive phrase. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "he sighed deeply" — στενάζω conveys a deep, possibly anguished exhalation. "Groaned" is too heavy; "sighed" alone is too light. "Sighed deeply" captures the emotional weight.

**Status:** Resolved. Blended rendering.

---

## 7:37 — "He-has-done all-[things] well" vs. "Everything he does is wonderful"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "He-has-done all-[things] well" | Literal; καλῶς πάντα πεποίηκεν. |
| LOGOS | "He has done all things well" | Formal; echoes Genesis 1:31 (LXX). |
| PNEUMA | "Everything he does is wonderful!" | Dynamic; "wonderful" for καλῶς. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "He has done all things well" — the echo of Genesis 1:31 ("God saw all that he had made, and it was very good") is essential. "Well" preserves this allusion; "wonderful" does not.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. The Genesis echo is theologically significant.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Occurrences | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| **Consistent theological vocabulary** (defile, scribes, blasphemy) | vv. 1-2, 5, 15, 22 | Traditional terms retained |
| **Difficult/obscure Greek** (πυγμῇ, κυνάρια diminutive) | vv. 3, 27 | Interpretive decisions with reasoning |
| **Vice list translation** | v. 22 | Individual decisions per term |
| **Rhetorical register** (ironic ΚΑΛΩΣ, woman's reply) | vv. 9, 28 | Moderate sarcasm preserved; over-colloquializing rejected |
| **Editorial parentheses** (v. 19 foods clean) | v. 19 | All three agree on interpretation; stylistic differences only |
| **Intertextual allusions** (Genesis 1 in v. 37) | v. 37 | Preserved where detectable |

**Overall Pattern Analysis:** Mark 7 presents unique translation challenges because of its sustained engagement with Jewish purity traditions, which require cultural knowledge to render accurately. The vice list in vv. 21-22 demanded individual word-level decisions. The most theologically significant translation decisions involve maintaining the "defile" vocabulary (κοινόω), handling the editorial parenthesis about clean foods (v. 19), and preserving the Genesis 1 echo in the crowd's acclamation (v. 37). PNEUMA's dynamic renderings are most valuable in this chapter for clarifying cultural practices (the Corban loophole, the "evil eye" idiom), but the consensus generally preserves traditional theological vocabulary.
